name: Mobility-Data Epidemic Modeling and Surveillance
description: This domain examines how human mobility data, particularly from mobile phone call records, can be leveraged to model and track epidemic spread in developing countries, as seen in studies on COVID-19 in Nigeria during flooding and malaria elimination planning. It combines agent-based and spatiotemporal disease models with mobility signals, while also weighing the challenges of accessing such data and the pitfalls of big data analysis exemplified by the Google Flu parable. Additional readings address forecasting techniques like Bayesian structural time series and measuring how epidemic alerts alter movement patterns.

background_concepts.concept: Call Detail Records (CDRs) as mobility proxy
background_concepts.why_needed: Several readings use mobile phone call records to infer population movement but assume the reader knows what CDRs are, how cell-tower pings approximate location, and their sampling/representativeness biases.

suggested_resources.title: Making the Most of Mobility Data (CDRs): A Guide for Policymakers
What are Call Detail Records (CDR data)? — FlowGeek

background_concepts.why_needed: The agent-based and spatiotemporal disease-spread papers build on standard susceptible-exposed-infectious-recovered dynamics as the underlying disease process, without explaining the compartments or transition rates.

The Scott nowcasting reading presents state-space/structural time-series forecasting and 'predicting the present' without introducing the underlying state-space decomposition or spike-and-slab regression basics assumed of the reader.

name: AI-Driven Vaccination and Immunization Uptake
description: This domain focuses on using AI and optimization to increase vaccine uptake, centered on the ADVISER system deployed in Nigeria for child vaccination intervention. It also covers fair vaccination strategies via influence maximization, sequential vaccine allocation under delayed feedback, and Bayesian modeling to correct for maternal recall bias in immunization rate estimates in India.

background_concepts.concept: Influence maximization / seed-set selection on networks
background_concepts.why_needed: The fair-vaccination paper frames vaccine promotion as choosing a 'seed set' to maximize spread over a social/contact network, assuming familiarity with diffusion models (independent cascade / linear threshold) and the greedy submodular approximation.

suggested_resources.title: WSDM 2018 Tutorial on Influence Maximization in Online Social Networks

name: Restless Bandits and Resource Allocation for Public Health Outreach
description: This domain applies restless bandits and reinforcement learning to allocate scarce health resources fairly and effectively in underserved communities, including index-based treatment allocation and Bayesian online approaches. Readings address maternal and child healthcare in non-Markovian settings, deep reinforcement learning for equitable resource distribution, and planning multiple epidemic interventions.

background_concepts.concept: Restless multi-armed bandits and the Whittle index
background_concepts.why_needed: These papers assume the reader understands RMABs, the passive/active arm formulation, indexability, and the Whittle index policy used to prioritize which beneficiaries to allocate limited health-worker attention to.

suggested_resources.title: Markovian restless bandits and index policies: A review (intro sections)

name: AI-Assisted Medical Imaging Diagnosis in Low-Resource Settings
description: This domain covers deep learning tools for diagnosing diseases from medical signals and images in low-resource areas, spanning cough-sound-based pulmonary screening, head and neck cancer triage, and pediatric oncology support for marginalized communities. Several readings focus specifically on detecting occupational lung diseases like silicosis and pneumoconiosis from chest X-rays.

background_concepts.concept: Convolutional neural networks for medical image classification
background_concepts.why_needed: The chest X-ray silicosis/pneumoconiosis and cancer-screening papers assume the reader understands CNN building blocks (convolution, pooling, fully-connected layers, AUC evaluation) used to classify medical images.

suggested_resources.title: A practical introduction to convolutional neural networks for medical professionals
Deep learning: CNNs for medical image classification (IMAIOS)

background_concepts.why_needed: SHIELD is described as a 'self-supervised hierarchical imaging framework,' assuming the reader knows how models learn representations from unlabeled images (pretext tasks, contrastive learning), which is essential in label-scarce clinical settings.

suggested_resources.title: Self-Supervised Learning: Self-Prediction and Contrastive Learning (NeurIPS 2021 tutorial slides)

name: Maternal, Child, and Reproductive Health Digital Systems
description: This domain explores digital tools and design approaches supporting maternal, child, and reproductive health in low-resource settings, including tele-triage for pregnant women in Kenya, self-directed learning for maternity workers, and OCR digitization of mother and child protection cards in India. It also addresses community cohesion among mothers in bandwidth-constrained settings, birth declaration to reduce child marriage in Bangladesh, tangible nutrition play in Peru, and causal analysis of contraceptive discontinuation.

background_concepts.concept: Causal inference from observational data (potential outcomes)
background_concepts.why_needed: The contraceptive-discontinuation paper applies causal inference to survey data, assuming familiarity with the potential-outcomes framework, counterfactuals, and confounding rather than teaching them.

suggested_resources.title: Causal Inference and Potential Outcomes (UCL PUBL0050 lecture notes)
Causal inference and effect estimation using observational data (glossary)

name: Climate-Health and Air Pollution Sensing
description: This domain integrates climate and air-quality sensing with health outcomes, covering climate-driven epidemic prediction, foundation models for emission sources and respiratory infection hotspots, and transformer-based air pollution source mapping in South Asia. Readings also address mitigating kiln industry pollution, wildfire smoke plume segmentation from satellite imagery, high-resolution road-network pollutant quantification, and extreme weather impacts on physical activity and sleep.

background_concepts.concept: Satellite remote sensing and multispectral imagery
background_concepts.why_needed: The emission-mapping, kiln-classifier, and wildfire-smoke papers rely on multispectral and geostationary satellite bands, spatial/spectral/temporal resolution tradeoffs, and image preprocessing that are assumed rather than explained.

suggested_resources.title: Fundamentals of Remote Sensing (NASA ARSET self-paced course)
Introduction to Spatial and Spectral Resolution: Multispectral Imagery (Earth Lab)

name: Clean Cooking and Household Energy Transitions
description: This domain addresses transitions to cleaner household cooking energy, using a health belief model and theory of change for service design in the slums of Kathmandu. It also assesses cooking energy behavior and the potential for shifting to electric cooking in informal urban settlements.

background_concepts.why_needed: The 'theory of change for cleaner cooking' paper explicitly builds a Health Belief Model for service design, assuming the reader knows its constructs (perceived susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, cues to action, self-efficacy).

suggested_resources.title: The Health Belief Model — Methods for Stress Management (Pressbooks)
Understanding the Health Belief Model — A Primer

name: Human-Centered Global Health Device Design
description: This domain focuses on designing and prototyping medical devices for global health, including life-saving warming technologies and stakeholder engagement through front-end device design. Readings also cover a usability study of an optical schistosomiasis diagnostic device in Nigeria and efforts to transform rural healthcare through technology crafts for the digitally underserved.

name: Designing for Stigmatized and Marginalized Care Recipients
description: This domain examines human-centered design for stigmatized and marginalized populations, including Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, caregivers of children with autism facing stigmatization, and adolescents managing Type 1 diabetes in LMICs. It also considers the paradoxes of India's vast population and untapped human resources within health care.

name: Secure and Agile mHealth Deployment in the Field
description: This domain addresses the operational, security, and organizational challenges of deploying mobile health systems in the field, including data classification for secure health data collection and security leadership where there is no CISO. It also covers applying agile principles to complex public health problems like malnutrition management and the adoption of mHealth services by internally displaced persons in Nigeria.

background_concepts.concept: CISO role and information security governance / data classification
background_concepts.why_needed: The paper's title and argument revolve around the absence of a Chief Information Security Officer and data-security classification schemes, assuming familiarity with security governance roles and data-classification mechanisms.

suggested_resources.title: DHIS2 Implementation Guide - security and data ownership section

name: Health Information System (HIS/DHIS2) Implementation in LMICs
description: This domain examines the deployment of open-source health information systems, particularly DHIS2, across low- and middle-income countries including Lao PDR, Mozambique, Lesotho, and Malawi. The readings explore how these systems can strengthen representation and inclusion of marginalized groups, while also documenting the complexity, distress, and social exclusion risks that arise during implementation. Technical themes include integrating community and facility health data through methods like machine learning and probabilistic record linkage.

background_concepts.why_needed: Readings in this domain assume familiarity with DHIS2's architecture, aggregate vs. case-based data, and the HISP implementation ecosystem.

name: mHealth Behavior Change and Frontline Health Worker Support
description: This domain covers the design and sustainability of mobile health interventions aimed at behavior change communication and supporting frontline health workers, with case studies from rural India and sub-Saharan Africa. It addresses both the strategic development of mHealth programs and the training and support practices needed to sustain them. It also includes technical approaches such as metric learning for handwritten digit recognition to expand the reach of voice-based communication platforms.

background_concepts.why_needed: The Uttar Pradesh case study develops a behavior change communication strategy assuming knowledge of the underlying behavior-change theory that structures such interventions.

suggested_resources.title: The Health Belief Model - Rural Health Promotion and Disease Prevention Toolkit

name: AI/ML for Clinical Prediction and Health Equity
description: This domain applies artificial intelligence and machine learning to clinical prediction tasks such as diabetes forecasting, chronic disease management through lab test response prediction, and suicide risk detection, alongside foundational methods like contrastive learning and self-supervised time-series clustering. A strong equity thread runs throughout, with readings on quantifying health inequalities induced by data and AI, achieving gender fairness in mental health prediction, and mapping vulnerability among disadvantaged urban populations. It also spans related predictive applications including insurance fraud detection and satellite-imagery-based poverty estimation.

background_concepts.why_needed: The subject-wise clustering and online contrastive-learning papers assume the reader understands how contrastive objectives (positive/negative pairs, embedding space) learn representations from largely unlabeled data.

name: Digital Technology in Pandemic Response and Risk Communication
description: This domain focuses on the social and technological dimensions of the COVID-19 pandemic in South Asia, examining impacts on poor and vulnerable groups in India and gender inequality among healthcare workers in Pakistan. It highlights the role of technology in enabling risk communication and community engagement, drawing on experiences from Bangladesh during the pandemic.

background_concepts.why_needed: The Bangladesh paper builds on the established public-health RCCE framework used during outbreaks, referencing it as a known institutional mechanism without introducing it.
